steps.title: Identify the two independent clauses
steps.content: The sentence contains two independent clauses: (1) “Leibniz wheel calculators were popular in the first half of the twentieth [blank]” and (2) “these ingenious devices were eventually replaced by electronic calculators.” The blank must provide punctuation and/or a connector that legally joins them while conveying that popularity gave way to replacement.

steps.title: Eliminate each choice and confirm the answer
steps.content:
• Choice A — century: no punctuation or conjunction between two independent clauses — produces a run-on sentence (fused clauses). ✗
  • Choice B — century,: a comma alone between two independent clauses — produces a comma splice. ✗
  • Choice C — century, but: a comma plus coordinating conjunction correctly joins two independent clauses; “but” also signals the contrast between the devices' popularity and their replacement. ✓
  • Choice D — century that: the subordinating word “that” turns the first clause into a fragment and creates an illogical, ungrammatical sentence. ✗
That confirms C is correct.
